schools.summary: Chrysostom: 'bewitched' implies envy and a malignant (evil) eye, but here it points not to the eye's natural power but to the deceivers' working on the Galatians by flattery, as the bewitched are harmed though they have done no wrong; Paul exclaiming at the foolish Galatians, asking who had bewitched them into disobeying the truth, when Christ crucified had been so plainly set before their eyes — the amazement at their seduction from the gospel (Paul marvelling that a people to whom Christ crucified had been preached so vividly, as if portrayed before their very eyes, could be charmed away from the truth by the Judaizers, treating their defection as a kind of bewitchment, so contrary was it to all they had seen and known).

themes: O foolish Galatians, who hath bewitched you
that ye should not obey the truth
Jesus Christ... evidently set forth, crucified
the amazement at their seduction

schools.summary: Gill: though there were many things he could put to them, Paul would ask this one question, which, if rightly attended to and honestly answered, must expose their folly and put an end to the controversy; Barnes: the design is to convince them by appeal to their own experience; Paul asking the one decisive question — whether they received the Spirit by the works of the law or by the hearing of faith — the appeal to their own experience (Paul resting the whole controversy on a single, unanswerable point: the Galatians had received the Spirit, with his gifts and graces, not when they kept the law but when they heard and believed the gospel, so their own experience proved the blessing came by faith, not by works).

themes: this only would I learn of you
Received ye the Spirit by the works of the law
or by the hearing of faith?
the appeal to their own experience

schools.summary: Gill: the wonder — 'is it possible you should be so stupid? and do you, or can you continue so?'; Barnes: Paul hardly thought it credible they could pursue such a course, having embraced the gospel so cordially and given such evidences of being under its influence; Paul asking, in astonishment, whether they are so foolish as to think that, having begun in the Spirit, they are now made perfect by the flesh — the absurdity of seeking perfection by the law after beginning by grace (Paul exposing the backward folly of a people who started their Christian life by the Spirit, through faith, and now imagined they could be brought to completion by the flesh, the works of the law, as if the lower could perfect what the higher began).

themes: are ye so foolish?
having begun in the Spirit
are ye now made perfect by the flesh?
the folly of seeking perfection by the law

schools.summary: Gill: the Galatians had suffered great reproach, many afflictions, and persecutions for the sake of the gospel (as all who embrace it must expect), which to those who persevere in the faith will not be in vain but followed with eternal life and glory (not that these are meritorious, the reward being not of debt but of grace); Paul asking whether they had suffered so many things in vain, adding 'if it be yet in vain' — the warning that apostasy would waste their sufferings (if they now abandoned the gospel for the law, all the persecutions endured for the faith would be thrown away and prove fruitless), yet tempering the warning with hope that they might still recover and not lose all.

themes: have ye suffered so many things in vain?
if it be yet in vain
apostasy would waste their sufferings
tempered with hope of recovery

schools.summary: Gill: the one who ministers the Spirit is not Paul or any minister (in whose power it does not lie to minister the Spirit), but God or Christ, who supplied and still supplied the grace of the Spirit, and the extraordinary gifts, through the preaching of the gospel, not the law; Paul again asking whether the One who supplies them the Spirit and works miracles among them does so by the works of the law or by the hearing of faith — the divine bestowal tied to faith not law (Paul pressing the same point a second time, now naming the ongoing supply of the Spirit and the working of miracles, insisting these divine operations came in connection with the believing reception of the gospel, never through law-keeping, so God's own dealings vindicated justification by faith).

themes: he that ministereth to you the Spirit, and worketh miracles
by the works of the law, or by the hearing of faith?
the divine bestowal tied to faith
God's dealings vindicating justification by faith

schools.summary: Gill: having shown that the special grace and gifts of the Spirit were received through the doctrine of faith not the preaching of the law, Paul passes by an easy transition to a further confirmation of justification by faith, producing the instance of Abraham — what Scripture says of him and the promise made to him; Paul citing that Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness (Genesis 15:6) — the great pattern of justification (Paul grounding his doctrine in the case of Abraham, the father of the faithful, whose faith, not his works, was reckoned/imputed to him for righteousness, so the very father of the Jewish nation, in whom they gloried, was himself justified by faith, the abiding pattern for all his children).

sides.position: By faith, not works — he believed God and his faith was reckoned/imputed to him for righteousness (Genesis 15:6), the pattern for all his children

themes: even as Abraham believed God
it was accounted to him for righteousness
the great pattern of justification
justified by faith, not works

schools.summary: Gill: this is a plain point, not to be contradicted or disputed — 'ye know'; Barnes: the inference that follows is that all who believe (and are justified in the same manner as Abraham) are the children of Abraham; Paul declaring that those who are of faith are the children of Abraham — the spiritual sonship of believers (Paul drawing the conclusion from Abraham's case: the true children of Abraham are not his natural descendants as such but those who share his faith, the 'of faith,' so believing Gentiles, no less than believing Jews, are Abraham's real seed, his heirs by the bond of faith rather than blood).

themes: they which are of faith
the same are the children of Abraham
the spiritual sonship of believers
Abraham's seed by faith, not blood

schools.summary: Gill: the Scripture is here, by a prosopopeia (personification), represented as foreseeing the event and speaking of it beforehand, designing God the author of Scripture, who foreknew that he would justify the Gentiles by faith (the Syriac rendering 'for seeing that God' foreknew); Paul declaring that the Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham, saying 'in thee shall all nations be blessed' — the gospel announced in the promise (the very promise to Abraham being itself an early preaching of the gospel, foreshowing that God's way of justifying the Gentiles would be by faith, so the doctrine the Judaizers opposed was no novelty but the ancient promise to the patriarch himself).

themes: the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith
preached before the gospel unto Abraham
in thee shall all nations be blessed
the gospel announced in the promise

schools.summary: Gill: this is the apostle's conclusion upon the whole, from the instance of Abraham and the promise made to him, and is an explanation of the preceding clause, to be taken in a limited sense — not of every individual, but only of those who are of the same faith with Abraham, believers in Christ who seek justification by faith in him and not by the works of the law; Paul concluding that those who are of faith are blessed together with faithful Abraham — the shared blessing of believers (all who believe, as Abraham believed, partaking of the same blessing he received, namely justification and all its benefits, so the believing Gentile is blessed in the company of the faithful patriarch, an heir of the promise made to him).

themes: they which be of faith
are blessed with faithful Abraham
the shared blessing of believers
heirs of the promise made to Abraham

schools.summary: Gill: Paul says not 'as many as were of the law' (the Jews, to whom it was given, some of whom believed and were blessed with Abraham), nor 'as many as do the works of the law' (for the works are to be done, though not to obtain righteousness), but 'as many as are of the works of the law' — those who seek justification and life by their own obedience; Paul declaring that as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse, since Scripture curses everyone who continues not in all things written in the law to do them (Deuteronomy 27:26) — the curse on the law-seeker (those who rest their hope of acceptance on keeping the law placing themselves under its curse, for the law demands perfect, perpetual obedience in all things and pronounces a curse on the least failure, so that, no man keeping it perfectly, all who seek life by it are condemned by it).

debates.question: Why are those 'of the works of the law' under a curse?

sides.position: The law demands perfect, perpetual obedience in all things and curses the least failure; since no one keeps it perfectly, all who seek justification by it are condemned by it

themes: as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse
cursed is every one that continueth not in all things
the curse on the law-seeker
the law demands perfect obedience

schools.summary: Gill: there are some justified — all God's elect in his eternal will and purpose to justify them upon Christ's righteousness, and all believers openly in the court of conscience under the Spirit's testimony — but no one is justified before God by the law; Paul declaring it evident that no man is justified by the law in God's sight, for the Scripture says (Habakkuk 2:4) 'the just shall live by faith' — the testimony of the prophet for justification by faith (Paul clinching the argument with Habakkuk's word, showing that the righteous man's life, his standing before God and his living from it, is by faith and not by law, so justification before God has always been, by the testimony of the prophets themselves, the gift received through faith).

themes: no man is justified by the law in the sight of God
it is evident
the just shall live by faith
the prophet's testimony to justification by faith

schools.summary: Gill: the law is not of faith — it does not consist of faith in Christ, nor require it, nor direct and encourage a man to live by it on Christ's righteousness; it is the gospel that reveals the righteousness of Christ and directs men to believe (and the law, though not of faith, is yet not of man but of God); Paul declaring that the law is not of faith but says 'the man that doeth them shall live in them' (Leviticus 18:5) — the contrasted ways of law and faith (law and faith opposite principles of acceptance: the law knowing nothing of believing but saying simply 'do and live,' promising life only to perfect doing, while faith receives life as a gift, so the two cannot be mixed, and to seek justification by both is to confound contraries).

debates.question: How do law and faith differ as principles of justification?

sides.position: They are opposite principles — the law says 'do and live' (promising life only to perfect obedience), faith receives life as a gift; the two cannot be mixed without confounding contraries

themes: and the law is not of faith
the man that doeth them shall live in them
the opposed principles of law and faith
'do and live' versus receiving life by faith

schools.summary: Gill: the Redeemer is Christ, the Son of God, appointed and called to this work, who agreed to it, spoken of in prophecy and come as such, having obtained eternal redemption — as man a near kinsman to whom the right of redemption belonged, and as God able to accomplish it; Paul declaring that Christ has redeemed believers from the curse of the law by being made a curse for them, as it is written 'cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree' (Deuteronomy 21:23) — the substitutionary bearing of the curse (Christ, to free his people from the law's curse under which all lay, taking that curse upon himself, becoming a curse in their stead, his death on the tree being the very form of accursedness named in the law, so that by bearing the penalty he exhausted it, delivering those for whom he was made a curse).

debates.question: How did Christ redeem us from the law's curse?

sides.position: By substitution — Christ was 'made a curse for us,' taking the law's curse upon himself in his people's stead (his death on the tree the form of accursedness named in the law), bearing and so exhausting the penalty

themes: Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law
being made a curse for us
cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree
the substitutionary bearing of the curse

schools.summary: Gill: 'the blessing of Abraham' is the same blessing Abraham enjoyed, justification by Christ's righteousness, and what was promised — that in his seed (Christ) the Gentiles should be blessed/justified; though it may comprise every spiritual blessing, it chiefly regards justification, the deliverance from the law's curse, which is the end of Christ's being made a curse; Paul stating the aim of Christ's being made a curse — that the blessing of Abraham might come on the Gentiles through Christ, and that believers might receive the promised Spirit through faith — the blessing and the Spirit secured by the cross (Christ bearing the curse to open to the Gentiles the blessing promised to Abraham and to secure for all believers the gift of the promised Spirit, both received not by law-works but through faith, the cross the channel of every gospel blessing).

themes: the blessing of Abraham might come on the Gentiles through Jesus Christ
receive the promise of the Spirit through faith
the twofold purpose of the cross
blessing and Spirit secured through faith

schools.summary: Gill: whereas he had called them 'foolish Galatians,' which might seem too harsh, Paul now softens his words and calls them 'brethren,' hoping still well of them, that they might be recovered, and imputing the fault rather to their leaders and teachers; Barnes: Paul draws an illustration from common usage; Paul, addressing them tenderly as brethren, drawing an illustration from human practice — that even a man's covenant, once confirmed/ratified, none annuls or adds to — the inviolability of a ratified covenant (Paul arguing from the lesser to the greater: if even a human agreement, once duly confirmed, is held sacred and cannot be set aside or altered, much more is God's confirmed covenant with Abraham unalterable, so the law given long after could not annul it).

themes: I speak after the manner of men
though it be but a man's covenant... if it be confirmed
no man disannulleth, or addeth thereto
the inviolability of a ratified covenant

schools.summary: Gill: the promises are those of the covenant of grace, exceeding great and precious, better than any other covenant's, all yea and amen in Christ, and chiefly of a spiritual nature (though temporal blessings come in a covenant way); Paul observing that the promises were made to Abraham and his seed, and that the singular 'seed' (not 'seeds, as of many') points to one, namely Christ — the seed of promise singularly Christ (Paul, weighing the very number of the word, drawing out that the covenant promise centred not on a multitude of descendants as such but on the one Seed, Christ, in whom the promise is fulfilled and through whom all, Jew and Gentile, become Abraham's children, so the inheritance flows through Christ and faith in him, not through the law).

sides.position: To show the promise centred on one Seed, Christ — in whom it is fulfilled and through whom all become Abraham's children, the inheritance flowing through Christ and faith, not the law

themes: to Abraham and his seed were the promises made
he saith not, And to seeds, as of many
to thy seed, which is Christ
the seed of promise singularly Christ

schools.summary: Barnes: the covenant was confirmed before of God, by God in his promise to Abraham, before the giving of the law; 'in Christ' means with respect to the Messiah, a covenant relating to him and promising he should descend from Abraham; Paul affirming that the covenant confirmed by God in Christ cannot be annulled by the law that came four hundred and thirty years afterward, so as to void the promise — the priority of the promise over the law (the covenant of grace, ratified by God to Abraham long before, cannot be cancelled or its promise made void by a law given centuries later, for what was first established and confirmed stands firm, and the temporary law could not overthrow the abiding promise).

themes: the covenant... confirmed before of God in Christ
the law, which was four hundred and thirty years after
cannot disannul... the promise
the priority of the promise over the law

schools.summary: Gill: 'the inheritance' means either eternal life and happiness in heaven (the gift of God through Christ, not attained by works of the law) or particularly the blessing of justification promised in the covenant to Abraham and his spiritual seed, even to the Gentiles, not obtained through obedience to the law of works; Paul arguing that if the inheritance is of the law it is no more of promise, but God gave it to Abraham by promise — the inheritance grounded in promise not law (Paul pressing the incompatibility: the inheritance, justification and eternal life, is either by promise or by law and cannot be both, and since God plainly bestowed it on Abraham as a free promise before the law existed, it rests on grace and promise, not law-keeping, which can add nothing).

themes: if the inheritance be of the law, it is no more of promise
but God gave it to Abraham by promise
the inheritance grounded in promise, not law
resting on grace

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: having spoken of the promise made to Abraham as the rule of justification, Paul anticipates the objection — to what purpose then was the law? Paul answering (why the law, if the inheritance is by promise?) — that it was added because of transgressions, until the promised Seed should come to whom the promise was made, and it was ordained by angels in the hand of a mediator (Moses) — the law's subordinate and temporary role (the law not given to justify but added alongside the promise to restrain and expose transgression, given to last only until Christ the Seed came, and given in a more removed manner through angels and a mediator, all marking it inferior and provisional, not the abiding ground of acceptance).

themes: wherefore then serveth the law?
it was added because of transgressions
till the seed should come... ordained by angels in the hand of a mediator
the law's subordinate and temporary role

schools.summary: Gill: a mediator supposes two parties he stands between, at a distance from or disagreeing with each other; where there is but one party there is no need of a mediator; Christ is the Mediator between God and men (the daysman who lays his hands on both, Job 9:33), and Moses was the mediator between God and the Israelites; Paul observing that a mediator is not a mediator of one but God is one — the contrast of the law's mediated giving with the promise's directness (Paul, in a notoriously compressed and variously explained saying, drawing a distinction between the law, which came through a mediator standing between two parties, God and the people, both bound by mutual conditions, and the promise, which God made of himself alone as one party freely giving, so the promise rests on God's single, unconditional grace, unlike the conditional, two-sided law).

debates.question: What does 'a mediator is not of one, but God is one' mean?

sides.position: It contrasts the law (given through a mediator between two parties, God and the people, mutually bound) with the promise (which God made of himself alone, one party freely giving), marking the promise's unconditional grace — a notoriously compressed verse with many proposed readings

themes: a mediator is not a mediator of one
but God is one
the contrast of mediated law and direct promise
the directness of grace

schools.summary: Gill: the objection — if the law was added because of transgressions and curses for them, and the inheritance is by promise, then the law seems against the promises, as if God contradicted himself in giving both; Paul replies, God forbid; Paul denying that the law is against God's promises, since if any law could have given life, righteousness would indeed have been by the law — the law and promise not contrary but the law unable to justify (Paul repelling the thought that law and promise are at odds, for they serve different ends under one God, and grounding the impossibility of justification by law in a plain fact: no law was ever given that could impart life, the law commanding but not enlivening, condemning but not justifying, so righteousness never could come by it).

themes: is the law then against the promises of God? God forbid
if there had been a law... which could have given life
the law not contrary to the promise
the law cannot give life or justify

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the Scripture' is meant either the law (the killing letter), or the whole Scripture, or God in it, who by and in it has shown, declared, and proved that all the individuals of human nature, Jews and Gentiles, and all that is in them and done by them, are under the power and dominion of sin, defiled by it and involved in its guilt; Paul declaring that Scripture has concluded (shut up) all under sin, so that the promise, by faith in Jesus Christ, might be given to those who believe — the universal imprisonment under sin opening the door to faith (Scripture, by exposing and convicting all as sinners and shutting them up under condemnation, leaving no escape by the law, and so driving the convicted to the only remaining door, the promise received by faith in Christ, that justification might come to all who believe).

themes: the scripture hath concluded all under sin
that the promise by faith of Jesus Christ might be given
shut up under sin, no escape by law
driven to the promise received by faith

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be understood not of the grace of faith (which was under the former dispensation as now, the Old Testament saints having the same Spirit and grace of faith, Adam, Abel, Enoch, Noah, Abraham believing in Christ and being justified by faith in his righteousness as we are), but of the doctrine of faith, the full gospel revelation; Paul declaring that before faith came believers were kept under the law, shut up unto the faith afterward to be revealed — the law as a custody until the gospel (before the full revelation of the gospel in Christ, the people of God held in ward under the law as in a kind of protective imprisonment, guarded and confined by its discipline, until the appointed time when the faith should be openly revealed and they brought into gospel liberty).

themes: before faith came, we were kept under the law
shut up unto the faith which should afterwards be revealed
the law as a custody until the gospel
held in ward until faith was revealed

schools.summary: Gill: the words should read 'the law was our schoolmaster unto Christ' (the words 'to bring us' being a supplement with nothing in the original); the law performed this office of a schoolmaster until the coming of Christ, showing that till then the church was in its minority, the Jews but children; Paul declaring that the law was their schoolmaster unto Christ, that they might be justified by faith — the disciplinary tutor preparing for Christ (the law serving as a 'schoolmaster,' the paidagogos, the guardian-tutor who disciplined and escorted a child, exercising God's people under its strict rule and, by exposing sin and the impossibility of self-righteousness, conducting them to Christ, that they might find in him the justification by faith the law itself could never give).

debates.question: What is the law's role as 'schoolmaster' (paidagogos)?

sides.position: The guardian-tutor who disciplined and escorted the child — the law exercised God's people under strict rule and, by exposing sin and the impossibility of self-righteousness, conducted them to Christ for justification by faith

themes: the law was our schoolmaster
to bring us unto Christ
that we might be justified by faith
the disciplinary tutor leading to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: since Christ, the object of faith, is come in the flesh, has fulfilled the law, and redeemed those who were under it from its bondage, curse, and condemnation; Barnes: after the scheme of salvation by faith is revealed, we are no longer under the pedagogue, kept in restraint and bondage; Paul declaring that, after faith is come, believers are no longer under a schoolmaster — the passing of the law's tutelage with the gospel (now that the full revelation of faith has come in Christ, who fulfilled the law and freed his people, the disciplinary tutelage of the law has reached its end and is laid aside, the people of God grown to maturity in Christ and no longer kept in the childhood bondage of the schoolmaster).

themes: after that faith is come
we are no longer under a schoolmaster
the passing of the law's tutelage
grown to maturity in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: they are children of God not by nature (as Christ is the Son, the only begotten of the Father, in such sense as neither angels nor men are), nor by creation (as Adam, all mankind, and the angels), but by divine adoption, an act of God's rich and sovereign grace putting them among the children; Paul declaring that they are all the children of God by faith in Christ Jesus — the adoption of all believers as sons (the privilege of being God's children belonging to all who believe, Gentile as well as Jew, received not by descent or law but by faith in Christ, so the whole company of believers, joined to the Son, share by grace the standing of sons of God).

themes: ye are all the children of God
by faith in Christ Jesus
the adoption of all believers as sons
received by faith, not descent or law

schools.summary: Gill: this way of speaking supposes some baptized in water may not be baptized into Christ (it is not to be imagined the churches consisted of baptized and unbaptized persons, but some, though baptized in water, were not baptized into Christ); those who are truly and rightly baptized into Christ have put him on; Paul declaring that as many as have been baptized into Christ have put on Christ — the putting on of Christ (those truly baptized into Christ, united to him, the inward reality the outward sign represents, having 'put on' Christ as a garment, clothed with him, with his righteousness for their justification and his image for their sanctification, the believer so joined to Christ that he is reckoned and renewed in him).

themes: as many of you as have been baptized into Christ
have put on Christ
the union with Christ in baptism
clothed with Christ's righteousness and image

schools.summary: Gill: there were such persons in being, and in the churches (which consisted of both), but the meaning is that there is no difference between them — in the business of justification and salvation it signified nothing whether a man was a circumcised Jew or an uncircumcised Greek, bond or free, male or female; Paul declaring that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, bond nor free, male nor female, for all are one in Christ Jesus — the oneness of all believers in Christ (the distinctions of nation, social rank, and sex, however real in their place, abolished as grounds of standing before God, for in the matter of justification, sonship, and the inheritance every believer stands on the same footing, all made one in Christ, the middle wall of partition broken down).

themes: there is neither Jew nor Greek... bond nor free... male nor female
ye are all one in Christ Jesus
the abolition of distinctions before God
every believer on the same footing

schools.summary: Gill: they are Christ's, not only by profession (calling themselves by his name) but by possession — Christ dwelling in their hearts by faith, and they having his Spirit as a spirit of regeneration and adoption; not Christ's by creation only, but by the Father's gift to him, the purchase of his blood, and the power of his grace making them willing; Paul concluding that if they are Christ's, then they are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise — the heirship of all who belong to Christ (the whole argument closing: those who are Christ's, the one true Seed, v.16, are in him themselves Abraham's seed and so heirs of the promise, the believing Gentile thereby brought into the full inheritance promised to Abraham, not by the law but by union with Christ and the bond of the promise).

themes: if ye be Christ's, then are ye Abraham's seed
and heirs according to the promise
the heirship of all who belong to Christ
heirs in the one true Seed, by union with Christ
